1 CV and future plans 1.1 Curriculum Vitae 1.1.1 Education VWO Gymnasium/Atheneum | Sep 2017 - Jun 2023 Comenius College, Hilversum Advanced secondary education: First 4 years Gymnasium, final 2 years Atheneum with Nature & Technology + Health profile. Expected graduation: 2027. 1.1.2 Skills PCR/qPCR Gel electrophoresis Molecular cloning DNA/RNA extraction NGS sequencing and pipelines Western blot / ELISA R, Bash scripting Metagenomic analysis 1.1.3 Languages Dutch: Native English: Advanced Spanish: Upper intermediate French: Intermediate German: Basic 1.1.4 Extra Competing at national/semi-professional level in tennis while maintaining top academic performance. Developed elite time management, discipline, and resilience combining intensive training/competition with rigorous STEM education. 1.1.5 Contact vonbalenandreas@fakegmail.com 1.2 Future plans Depending on what the future brings, my goals are either to pursue a professional tennis career or to work on complex biomedical challenges. In tennis, I already have a solid foundation in technique, tactics, physical conditioning, and mentality. These skills can improve significantly if I have more time and space to develop them full-time. My ultimate goal would be to earn a living from the sport. Achieving that requires not only performance on the court, but also strong skills off the court, such as time management, discipline, and unwavering motivation. These are qualities I will continue to develop. Another path I see for myself is working on complex biomedical questions. This could be in a laboratory, where I would apply the experimental skills I developed during my studies, or outside a laboratory, where I would use my analytical skills to work with existing data and help answer research questions. In both settings, I have built a broad base of skills that will allow me to get started, but I will still need to develop further, especially in collaborating with colleagues in a professional environment, which is different from working with fellow students. I also expect that dealing with setbacks in a workplace will be different from doing so in a university setting, so that is another area in which I want to grow.

2 Personal assignment I chose to learn variant annotation because it is a relevant bioinformatics skill for working with genomic data in biomedical research. Variant annotation adds biological context to genetic variants by linking them to genes, predicted functional effects, and external databases. This makes it possible to move from raw variant lists to meaningful biological interpretation. As I would like to develop skills that are useful in both laboratory and data analysis settings, this topic fits well with my future plans. 2.1 Planning 2.1.1 Week 1: Learning the basics Learning the theory behind variant annotation. Exploring what VCF files are and how annotation works. Following a tutorial on one (or more) annotation tool(s) or workflow(s). Identifying which software/package to use for my own analysis. 2.1.2 Week 2: Applying the skill Finding a new dataset or example VCF file. Annotating the variants using the method/tool. Filtering or prioritizing the variants based on functional impact. Checking whether the output makes biological sense. 2.1.3 Week 3: Writing up the assignment Cleaning up the code and making it reproducible. Writing a short explanation of the workflow and choices. Summarizing what I learned and what was challenging. Preparing the final report in RMarkdown or another clear format. 2.2 Development
